Subword ELMo

Jiangtong Li 1 , ∗ , Hai Zhao 1 , 2 , 3 , † , Zuchao Li 1 , 2 , 3 , Wei bi 4 , Xiaojiang Liu 4 1 Department of Computer Science and Engineering,

dc229754-e62d-404f-9178-14b0f5041999.label: text_chunk
dc229754-e62d-404f-9178-14b0f5041999.text: Shanghai Jiao Tong University 2 Key Laboratory of Shanghai Education Commission for Intelligent Interaction and Cognitive Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China 3 MoE Key Lab of Artificial Intelligence, AI Institute, Shanghai Jiao Tong University 4 Tencent AI Lab, Shenzhen, China jiangtongli1997@gmail.

fa4b55ad-f601-4fa0-b846-55982f2a0f19.text:
com, zhaohai@cs.sjtu.edu.cn, charlee@sjtu.edu.cn, { victoriabi, kieranliu } @tencent.com

Abstract

Embedding from Language Models (ELMo) has shown to be effective for improving many natural language processing (NLP) tasks, and ELMo takes character information to compose word representation to train language mod- els.

8a66c9f8-6d57-421b-89df-d69af9f3508a.label: text_chunk
8a66c9f8-6d57-421b-89df-d69af9f3508a.text: However, the character is an insufficient and unnatural linguistic unit for word repre- sentation. Thus we introduce Embedding from Subword-aware Language Models (ESuLMo) which learns word representation from sub- words using unsupervised segmentation over words.

79ccf047-67ab-445f-b93f-a93d953e07ea.label: text_chunk
79ccf047-67ab-445f-b93f-a93d953e07ea.text:
We show that ESuLMo can enhance four benchmark NLP tasks more effectively than ELMo, including syntactic dependency parsing, semantic role labeling, implicit dis- course relation recognition and textual entail- ment, which brings a meaningful improvement over ELMo.

1 Introduction

Recently, pre-trained language representation has shown to be useful for improving many NLP tasks ( Peters et al. , 2018 ; Devlin et al.

a84f1934-d620-4169-a42a-f6ccb4256c0b.label: text_chunk
a84f1934-d620-4169-a42a-f6ccb4256c0b.text:
, 2018 ; Radford et al. , 2018 ; Howard and Ruder , 2018 ). Em- beddings from Language Models (ELMo) ( Peters et al. , 2018 ) is one of the most outstanding works, which uses a character-aware language model to augment word representation.

An essential challenge in training word-based language models is how to control vocabulary size for better rare word representation.

7531ac47-5e7e-4fad-b38d-809b6162af85.label: text_chunk
7531ac47-5e7e-4fad-b38d-809b6162af85.text:
No matter how large the vocabulary is, rare words are always in- sufficiently trained. Besides, an extensive vocab- ulary takes too much time and computational re- source for the model to converge. Whereas, if the vocabulary is too small, the out-of-vocabulary
(OOV) issue will harm the model performance heavily ( Jozefowicz et al. , 2016 ).

d0d9588b-16c3-4a8f-a5e5-b7777adf9997.label: text_chunk
d0d9588b-16c3-4a8f-a5e5-b7777adf9997.text: To obtain effec- tive word representation, Jozefowicz et al. ( 2016 ) introduce character-driven word embedding us- ing convolutional neural network (CNN) ( LeCun et al. , 1998 ), following the language model in Kim et al. ( 2016 ) for deep contextual representation.

1f71a3b2-b81d-4246-9fc1-f2fecab24b72.label: text_chunk
1f71a3b2-b81d-4246-9fc1-f2fecab24b72.text: However, there is potential insufficiency when modeling word from characters which hold lit- tle linguistic sense, especially, the morphologi- cal source ( Fromkin et al. , 2018 ). Only 86 char- acters(also included some common punctuations) are adopted in English writing, making the input too coarse for embedding learning.

89617f8d-287f-45f1-a48f-769e57c3ede9.label: text_chunk
89617f8d-287f-45f1-a48f-769e57c3ede9.text: As we argue that for better representation from a refined granu- larity, word is too large and character is too small, it is natural for us to consider subword unit be- tween character and word levels.Splitting a word into subwords and using them to augment the word representation may recover the latent syntactic or semantic information ( Bo- janowski et al. , 2017 ).

391fc076-0040-4183-b2a7-cee5d85fca94.label: text_chunk
391fc076-0040-4183-b2a7-cee5d85fca94.text: For example, useless- ness could be split into the following subwords: < use , less , ness > . Previous work usually con- siders linguistic knowledge-based methods to to- kenize each word into subwords (namely, mor- phemes) ( Luong et al. , 2010 , 2013 ; Bhatia et al. , 2016 ). However, such treatment may encounter three main inconveniences.

3701cb4b-59d6-4b60-8d3d-6bac8c93759c.label: text_chunk
3701cb4b-59d6-4b60-8d3d-6bac8c93759c.text: First, the subwords from linguistic knowledge, typically including the morphological suffix, prefix, and stem, may not be suitable for a targeted NLP task ( Banerjee and Bhattacharyya , 2018 ) or mislead the representa- tion of some words, like the meaning of under- stand cannot be formed by under and stand .

4c816eae-33a7-468e-88b4-44db1d3607fb.label: text_chunk
4c816eae-33a7-468e-88b4-44db1d3607fb.text: Sec- ond, linguistic knowledge, including related anno- tated lexicons or corpora, may not even be avail- able for a specific low-resource language. Due to these limitations, we focus on computationally motivated subword tokenization approaches in this work.∗ ∗ Work done when Jiangtong Li was interning at Ten- cent AI Lab. † Corresponding author.

217f5146-8dd7-42ba-bd8c-c7c5e850c89c.label: text_chunk
217f5146-8dd7-42ba-bd8c-c7c5e850c89c.text: This paper was par- tially supported by National Key Research and Develop- ment Program of China (No. 2017YFB0304100) and Key Projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1836222 and No. 61733011).Figure 1: Model Structure (We replace the input block by the Word Segmentation block.)

fc3aaee0-471c-4a66-84d7-bd9c26fc0048.label: text_chunk
fc3aaee0-471c-4a66-84d7-bd9c26fc0048.text: In this paper, we propose Embedding from Subword-aware Language Models (ESuLMo), which takes subword as input to augment word representation and release a sizeable pre-trained language model research communities.

4fe48c3c-6a74-497c-b402-6a4d5f295c56.label: text_chunk
4fe48c3c-6a74-497c-b402-6a4d5f295c56.text:
Evalu- ations show that the pre-trained language mod- els of ESuLMo outperform all RNN-based lan- guage models, including ELMo, in terms of PPL and ESuLMo outperforms state-of-the-art results in three of four downstream NLP tasks.

2 General Language Model

The overall architecture of our subword-aware language model shows in Figure 1 .

9f72a62d-00d8-4fc1-b3b4-10a7af7b7480.label: text_chunk
9f72a62d-00d8-4fc1-b3b4-10a7af7b7480.text: It consists of four parts, word segmentation, word-level CNN, highway network and sentence-level RNN.Given a sentence S = { W 1 , W 2 , ..., W n } , we first use a segmentation algorithm to divide each word into a sequence of subwords ( Sennrich et al. , 2016 ; Kudo , 2018 ).

03ce0344-6875-4133-a9e8-3688f94687ee.label: text_chunk
03ce0344-6875-4133-a9e8-3688f94687ee.text:
M i = { x i, 1 , x i, 2 , ..., x i,m } = f ( X i ) (1)
where M i is the output of the segmentation algo- rithm, x i,j is the subword unit and f represents the segmentation algorithm. Then a look-up table is applied to transform the subword sequence into subword embeddings ( Mikolov et al. , 2013 ).

bdbba38f-92f9-4324-bd92-32687de376ac.label: text_chunk
bdbba38f-92f9-4324-bd92-32687de376ac.text:
To further augment the word representation from the subwords, we apply a narrow convolution between subword embeddings and several kernels.y i = Concat ( g ( M i , K 1 ) , g ( M i , K 2 ) , ... ) , (2)
where Concat is the concatenation operation for all the input vectors, K i is convolution kernel and g is CNN-MaxPooling operation.A highway network ( Srivastava et al.

398dd534-f183-428d-be63-64854f89e0d2.label: text_chunk
398dd534-f183-428d-be63-64854f89e0d2.text: , 2015 ) is then applied to the output of CNN. A bidi- rectional long short-term memory network (Bi- LSTM) ( Hochreiter and Schmidhuber , 1997 ) gen- erates the hidden states for the given sentence rep- resentation in forward and backward. Finally, the probability of each token is calculated by applying an affine transformation to all the hidden states fol- lowed by a Sof tM ax function.

ab8b5326-7c74-4cf2-a685-d5d97e678004.label: text_chunk
ab8b5326-7c74-4cf2-a685-d5d97e678004.text:
During the train- ing, our objective is to minimize the negative log- likelihood of all training samples.To apply our pre-trained language models to other NLP tasks, we combine the input vector and the last layer’s hidden state of the Bi-LSTM to rep- resent each word.

3 Subword from Unsupervised Segmentation

To segment subwords from a word, we adopt the generalized unsupervised segmentation frame- work proposed by Zhao and Kit ( 2008 ).

d0ced67c-fd4e-4624-9e4d-730b5352459d.label: text_chunk
d0ced67c-fd4e-4624-9e4d-730b5352459d.text:
The gen- eralized framework can be divided into two col- locative parts, goodness measure (score), which evaluates how likely a subword is to be a proper one, and a segmentation or decoding algorithm.

 For the sake of simplicity, we choose frequency as the goodness score and two representative decod- ing algorithms, byte pair encoding (BPE) ( Sen- nrich et al.

e7bf66a8-fadc-43fa-bf35-cd7746a90ebf.label: text_chunk
e7bf66a8-fadc-43fa-bf35-cd7746a90ebf.text:
, 2016 ) which uses a greedy decoding algorithm and unigram language model (ULM) ( Kudo , 2018 ) which adopts a Viterbi-style decod- ing algorithm.For a group of character sequences, the working procedure of BPE is as follows:

• All the input sequences are tokenized into a se- quence of single-character subwords.

f3b07978-a997-492f-a4be-07f2ee858ee2.label: text_chunk
f3b07978-a997-492f-a4be-07f2ee858ee2.text:
• Repeatedly,

 we calculate the frequencies of all bigrams and merge the bigram with the highest one until we get the desired subword vocabulary.ULM is proposed based on the assumption that each subword occurs independently. The working procedure of ULM segmentation is as follows.

• Heuristically make a reasonably large seed vo- cabulary from the training corpus.

5289a491-6597-438c-bc9c-73a768d12494.label: text_chunk
5289a491-6597-438c-bc9c-73a768d12494.text:
• Iteratively, the probability of each subword is estimated by the expectation maximization (EM) algorithm and the top η % subwords with the high- est probabilities are kept. Note that we always keep the single character in subword vocabulary to avoid out-of-vocabulary.For a specific dataset, the BPE algorithm keeps
the same segmentation for the same word in dif- ferent sequences, whereas ULM cannot promise such segmentation.

6cdb0337-ec44-4f96-ade8-58e194e7076d.label: text_chunk
6cdb0337-ec44-4f96-ade8-58e194e7076d.text:
Both segmentation algorithms have their strengths, Sennrich et al. ( 2016 ) show that BPE can fix the OOV issue well, and Kudo ( 2018 ) proves that ULM is a subword regulariza- tion which is helpful in neural machine translation.

4 Experiments

The ESuLMo is evaluated in two ways, task in- dependent and task dependent.

4b261921-8e42-4f75-ae32-1c7649b98934.label: text_chunk
4b261921-8e42-4f75-ae32-1c7649b98934.text:
For the former, we examine the perplexity of the pre-trained lan- guage models. For the latter, we examine on four benchmark NLP tasks, syntactic dependency pars- ing, semantic role labeling, implicity discourse re- lation recognition, and textual entailment.

4.2 Downstream Tasks

While applying our pre-trained ESuLMo to other NLP tasks, we have two different strategies: (1) Fine-tuning our ESuLMo while training other NLP tasks; (2) Fixing our ESuLMo while training other NLP tasks. During the experiment, we find there is no significant difference between these two strategies.

3d8f84be-01c0-45bb-ac43-deb3e20fdb97.label: text_chunk
3d8f84be-01c0-45bb-ac43-deb3e20fdb97.text:
However, the first strategy con- sumes much more resource than the second one.

 Therefore, we choose the second strategy to con- duct all the remaining experiments.We apply ESuLMo to four benchmark NLP tasks. And we choose the fine-tuned model by val- idation set and report the results in the test set.

9e38556f-5abb-480d-af22-2e9a5fffc3cb.label: text_chunk
9e38556f-5abb-480d-af22-2e9a5fffc3cb.text:
The comparisons in Table 2 show that ESuLMo outper- forms ELMo significantly in all tasks and achieves the new state-of-the-art result in three of four tasks 4 .

Syntactic Dependency Parsing (SDP) is to disclose the dependency structure over a given sentence. Dozat and Manning ( 2017 ) use a Bi- LSTM encoder and a bi-affine scorer to deter- mine the relationship between two words in a sen- tence.

The Stanford Natural Language In- ference (SNLI) corpus ( Bowman et al. , 2015 ) provides approximately 550K hypothesis/premise pairs. Our baseline adopts ESIM ( Chen et al.

Our ESuLMo outperforms ELMo by 0.8% in terms of accuracy. Though our performance does not reach the state-of-the-art, it is second-best in all single models according to the SNLI leaderboard 5 .

5 Discussion

Subword Vocabulary Size Tables 1 and 2 show the performance of ESuLMo drops with the vo- cabulary size increases 6 . We explain the trend that neural network pipeline especially CNN would fail to capture necessary details of building word embeddings as more subwords are introduced.

8570dd73-8d98-4229-bc81-b09afb3730c9.label: text_chunk
8570dd73-8d98-4229-bc81-b09afb3730c9.text: Subword Segmentation Algorithms Tables 1 and 2 show that ESuLMo based on both ULM and BPE segmentation with 500 subwords outperform the original ELMo, and BPE is consistently bet- ter than ULM on all evaluations under the same settings. We notice that BPE can give static sub- word segmentation for the same word in differ- ent sentences, while ULM cannot. It suggests that ESuLMo is sensitive to segmentation consistency.

6 Conclusion

In this paper, we present Embedding from Subword-aware Language Model (ESuLMo). The experiments show that the language models of ESuLMo outperform all RNN-based language models, including ELMo, in terms of PPL. The
